content:
Hi, I'd like to begin writing an out-of-tree kernel module but I'm having some trouble setting up the environment. I have Ubuntu running inside a virtual machine. I would like to be able to code inside this machine, build, and insert into the VM. All the guides I'm finding seem to be loading it into qemu, but as this is already a VM, it seems excessive.
How much of the quickstart guide should I be following, and at what point do I need to do something different?
You could run qemu inside your VM. You probably do not want to insert your modules into the kernel of the VM you are using to write code and build. It might crash.
It's a virtual machine, I'm not worried about it crashing.
It will likely be easier to develop in-tree.

How much of the quickstart guide should I be following, and at what point do I need to do something different?

If you are referring to the guide in Documentation/rust/, that one is mainly about setting up the toolchain.
For out-of-tree modules, you can see a basic template at https://github.com/Rust-for-Linux/rust-out-of-tree-module
I can't get rust-analyzer to work. Adding the M=pwd like in the example makes it error. image.png
Could you please paste the terminal text instead of an image? If you really wrote M=pwd, it won't work, it should be a command substitution like M=$(pwd)

user@DESKTOP:~/rust-stuff/rust-out-of-tree-module$ make -C /home/user/rust-stuff/linux M=$(pwd) rust-analyzer
make: Entering directory '/home/user/rust-stuff/linux'
make: *** No rule to make target 'rust-analyzer'. Stop.
make: Leaving directory '/home/user/rust-stuff/linux'
Yeah, sorry, you need to apply this patch to your kernel: https://lore.kernel.org/rust-for-linux/20230121052507.885734-1-varmavinaym@gmail.com/
I have temporarily updated the README.md until that patch lands (which is waiting for v3).
Thanks it works. I noticed the out of tree init function is also outdated, the examples under samples/rust use fn init(name: &'static CStr, module: &'static ThisModule) -> Result<Self> {

I'm doing this for a uni assignment, it wants me to create a /proc/timer entry. I saw several PRs that add some support for this, but apparently procfs is deprecated. Are there some alternatives or is it not possible yet?
You should instead add it somewhere in /sys if allowed by the assignment.
I don't think they'll be too strict about it. Do you know which sample code I should look at to get an idea of how to do it?
Im currently working on sysfs abstractions, maybe those can help you (but right now only for device drivers)
